title: How Trauma Creates Disconnection from Hunger and Fullness
researcherTopic: radical acceptance, RAIN technique, Buddhist psychology
metaDescription: Trauma can sever the connection to your body's signals of hunger and fullness. Learn why this happens and how to gently rebuild interoceptive awareness.
imageDescription: A pair of gentle hands hold a simple, empty ceramic bowl. Warm, luminous morning light streams in from a nearby window, casting soft shadows and illuminating the subtle textures of the handmade pottery. The scene evokes a sense of quiet contemplation, nourishment, and the safety of a new beginning.
finalH2: The Slow Return to Embodied Trust
htmlContent:
Tara Brach, in her work on radical acceptance, often speaks of the trance of unworthiness, a state where we disconnect from our own lived experience—a disconnection that frequently begins not in the mind, but in the body's most fundamental signals, like the simple call of hunger or the quiet whisper of being full. This isn't a conscious choice, but a deeply ingrained survival strategy, a way the nervous system learns to navigate a world that felt unsafe, unpredictable, or overwhelming. We learn to live in our heads because the body became a territory of pain, confusion, or betrayal. The language of our own biology becomes foreign, a dialect we were forced to forget in order to endure.

The Body as a Forgotten Language

Interoception is the technical term for this internal language, the intricate network of signals that allows us to feel and interpret our body's inner state—our heart rate, our breath, the need for rest, and, most primally, our hunger and satiety. It is the foundation of our felt sense of self, the very bedrock of our emotional and physical experience. For a securely attached child, this language is learned effortlessly, with hunger cries being met with nourishment and comfort, creating a seamless feedback loop of trust between internal cues and external reality. But when trauma enters the equation, particularly the developmental and relational trauma that defines C-PTSD, this entire system is thrown into disarray. The body, instead of being a source of reliable information and safety, becomes a landscape of potential threats.

When a child's environment is characterized by neglect, chaos, or abuse, the body's signals become entangled with fear, anxiety, and the desperate need for safety. A pang of hunger might be followed not by a warm meal, but by the terrifying sound of a parent's angry footsteps, or the profound emptiness of being alone. The body learns that its needs are not only unmet but potentially dangerous to express. In order to survive, the developing nervous system makes a brilliant, albeit costly, adaptation: it begins to mute these internal signals. Disconnection becomes a form of self-preservation. If feeling your body means feeling terror, then the most intelligent thing to do is to stop feeling your body. We retreat into the attic of the intellect, becoming observers of our lives rather than participants, all the while wondering why we feel so disconnected from ourselves and the world. The question of what interoceptive awareness truly means becomes a distant academic inquiry rather than a lived reality.

Hunger and Fullness: More Than Just Fuel Gauges

In a regulated nervous system, hunger and fullness are simple, elegant signals—the body's way of maintaining homeostasis. They are the biological equivalents of a fuel gauge, indicating a need for energy or signaling that the tank is full. But through the lens of trauma, these signals are stripped of their neutrality. They become encoded with layers of relational meaning, emotional memory, and existential threat. Hunger ceases to be a simple request for food; it can become a terrifying echo of profound neglect, a reminder of a time when needs were a source of danger or abandonment. Fullness is no longer a gentle sense of satisfaction; it might feel like a loss of control, a vulnerability, or a trigger for deep-seated shame about taking up space.

This is because for a developing human, the experience of being fed is inextricably linked with the experience of being safe, seen, and soothed. The process of attunement, where a caregiver accurately perceives and responds to a child's needs, is the foundation of a secure attachment and a regulated nervous system. When this process is disrupted—when a cry of hunger is met with anger, indifference, or inconsistency—the child's brain learns a devastating equation: my needs are a threat to my connection with my caregiver, and therefore a threat to my survival. The nervous system, in its infinite wisdom, begins to distort or sever the connection to those internal cues. It learns to predict danger, not nourishment. It anticipates abandonment, not attunement. This is the cruel genius of adaptation. The body does what it must to survive the unsurvivable, even if it means sacrificing its own internal compass.

The brain is prediction machinery. Anxiety is just prediction running without a stop button.

The Spectrum of Disconnection: From Numbness to Noise

This disconnection from hunger and fullness doesn't sign in a single, uniform way; it exists on a spectrum, often oscillating between two poles: somatic numbness and overwhelming noise. On one end of the spectrum is a profound numbness, an inability to feel or interpret the body's signals at all. This is the person who can go an entire day without realizing they haven't eaten, only to be hit with a sudden wave of dizziness or irritability. For them, the body is a distant, muted landscape. The signals of hunger are so faint they are easily ignored, overridden by the demands of work, caregiving, or the simple, consuming task of getting through the day. This numbness is not a personal failing but a deeply learned defense, a form of dissociation that allowed the self to endure a reality where bodily needs were irrelevant or dangerous. It is a fortress built to protect from overwhelming sensation, but its walls are so thick that even the most basic, life-sustaining messages cannot get through.

For further reading and professional resources, visit the EMDR International Association.

On the other end of the spectrum is a chaotic internal noise, where the signals are constant, overwhelming, and impossible to decipher. This can indicator as a perpetual, gnawing anxiety in the stomach that is mistaken for hunger, leading to compulsive eating in an attempt to soothe a feeling that food can't touch. It can also appear as a hyper-awareness of every internal sensation, where the slightest feeling of fullness is interpreted as a sign of being bloated, sick, or out of control. In my years of working in this territory, I've sat with many people who describe this state as being trapped in a room where every alarm is blaring at once. There is no clarity, only a cacophony of sensation that offers no reliable information. Right here, practices like Tara Brach's RAIN (Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture) technique can become so vital, not as a way to fix or silence the noise, but as a way to begin relating to it with a degree of compassionate attention. We learn to sit with the storm without becoming the storm. This work is complex, and it's essential to ground our understanding in authoritative research on the nervous system, which confirms the profound impact of trauma on these bodily systems. The process often involves navigating the extremes of somatic numbness and chaotic sensation as we slowly find our way back to a more integrated state.

The Architecture of Survival: Why the Body Adapts This Way

It is tempting, and entirely understandable, to view this disconnection from our body's most basic signals as a flaw, a brokenness, or a personal failure. We judge ourselves for not being able to "eat intuitively" or for feeling so out of touch with our own needs. But one of the most profound shifts in healing complex trauma is to begin to see these adaptations not as pathologies, but as the architecture of survival. The disconnection was not a mistake; it was a masterpiece of self-preservation crafted by a nervous system doing exactly what it was designed to do: keep us alive in the face of overwhelming threat. If, as a child, the feeling of hunger consistently led to the experience of emotional abandonment or physical danger, then the most intelligent and adaptive response was to sever the connection to that feeling. The body learned to protect the self by sacrificing its own voice.

This reframing is not about excusing or bypassing the pain of the present-day consequences. The numbness, the chaos, the disordered eating patterns—they are all sources of real suffering. But by understanding their origin as a form of protection, we can begin to approach them with a sense of reverence and curiosity rather than judgment and shame. We can start to see the body not as an adversary that is betraying us, but as a loyal ally that has been holding an impossible burden for a very long time. A client once described this realization as feeling like she had been angry at a loyal guard dog for barking, without ever realizing it had been protecting her from intruders her whole life. The work then becomes not about silencing the dog, but about slowly, patiently helping it to understand that the war is over.

There is no version of growth that doesn't involve the dissolution of something you thought was permanent.

This process requires us to gently challenge the very identity structures that were built around this disconnection. For many, the identity of being someone who is "in control," "unflappable," or "low-maintenance" is a direct result of this somatic muting. To begin to feel the body's needs again is to risk dismantling a core part of who we believe ourselves to be. It is a journey into the unknown, a willingness to let go of the familiar armor of the intellect and to step into the messy, unpredictable, and profoundly alive world of sensation. We must be willing to look beyond the surface-level explanations and behaviors, because often, the most intricate defenses are the ones that keep us stuck in familiar loops of suffering.

Complexity is the ego's favorite hiding place.

Re-establishing the Connection: A Practice of Gentle Inquiry

So how does one begin to rebuild a bridge to a continent that has been submerged for decades? The answer, frustratingly and beautifully, is slowly, and with a quality of attention that is radically different from the problem-solving mindset that dominates our culture. The impulse is to want to "fix" the disconnection, to find the right technique or protocol that will restore perfect interoceptive awareness. But this very impulse is often a subtle form of the same intellectual dominance that created the disconnection in the first place. The path back to the body is not one of conquest, but of courtship. It is a practice of gentle, non-demand inquiry, a willingness to listen to the silence and the noise without needing either to change.

This practice can begin in the smallest of moments. It might look like pausing for just ten seconds before the first bite of a meal, not to judge the food or to rehearse a diet rule, but simply to notice what is present in the body. Is there a hollowness in the stomach? A tension in the jaw? A sense of urgency? The goal is not to get a clear answer, but to create a space for an answer to potentially arise. It could be the practice of placing a hand on the stomach after eating and simply feeling the sensation of contact, without labeling it as "bloated" or "full." It is a radical act of presence in a world that rewards constant distraction. In these small, repeated moments of turning toward the body with curiosity, we begin to send a new message to the nervous system: "I am here. I am listening. You are safe to speak."

The aim is not to achieve some mythical state of perfect attunement where every bodily signal is received with crystal clarity. That is a goal born of the same perfectionism that often fuels the trauma response itself. The true practice is simply to cultivate the willingness to listen at all. It is about shifting the fundamental orientation from one of control to one of companionship. We are not trying to manage the body; we are learning to be with it. This is the heart of the practice of gentle inquiry, a process that honors the body's wisdom, even when that wisdom speaks in whispers, riddles, or roars.

Stop pathologizing normal human suffering. Not everything requires a diagnosis.

The Slow Return to Embodied Trust

It is crucial to hold this entire process with a profound sense of patience, recognizing that the return to embodied trust is not a linear progression but a slow, meandering spiral. There will be days of clarity, where the signals of hunger and fullness feel distinct and reliable, followed by days or weeks where the old numbness or noise returns with a vengeance. This is not a sign of failure; it is the nature of healing. We are not simply learning a new skill; we are fundamentally rewiring a nervous system that has been shaped by years, or even decades, of perceived threat. We are rebuilding a relationship with a part of ourselves that has been exiled, and relationships are never built on a predictable timeline.

This journey is about much more than simply learning to eat when you are hungry and stop when you are full. That is the domain of pop-culture intuitive eating, a concept that, while well-intentioned, often fails to account for the profound rupture that trauma creates. For someone with a history of C-PTSD, the invitation to "just listen to your body" can feel like a cruel joke. It is like telling someone who is colorblind to simply appreciate the sunset. The work is not to try harder to see the colors, but to first acknowledge the reality of the colorblindness and to understand, with compassion, how it came to be. The work is to rebuild the very sensory apparatus that allows for that perception in the first place. This is a much deeper, more sacred act of reconciliation.

It involves grieving the loss of that innate connection, the childhood that should have been filled with attunement and safety. It requires a willingness to feel the deep well of sadness for the body that has been starved, or stuffed, or ignored in a desperate attempt to feel something, or to feel nothing at all. This is the slow, painstaking work of reclaiming the body as home, not just as a vessel to be managed or a problem to be solved. And as we undertake this journey, we find that the path has been illuminated by a wealth of clinical and contemplative wisdom, even if it remains largely outside the mainstream conversation. The insights from decades of clinical studies converge with the core tenets of ancient contemplative traditions, pointing to a truth that is both simple and profound: the body keeps the score, and healing happens when we finally learn to read its language.

The research is clear on this, and it contradicts almost everything popular culture teaches.

What happens next is not up to the research, or the framework, or the therapist, or this article. What happens next is up to the person reading this sentence right now. And that person already has everything they need to take the next step. The only thing missing is the decision.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why can't I just "listen to my body" like everyone says?

For individuals with a history of complex trauma, the channels of communication with the body have often been intentionally severed as a survival mechanism. The invitation to simply "listen" can feel invalidating because it overlooks the reality that the signals may be muted, chaotic, or encoded with danger. The work is not just to listen, but to first create the conditions of safety and presence that allow the body's true voice to emerge from behind decades of protective silence or noise.

Is it possible to fully heal this disconnection?

Healing this disconnection is a profound possibility, but it may not look like a return to a state that existed before the trauma; rather, it is the creation of a new, more conscious relationship with the body. It involves moving from a state of unconscious disconnection to one of conscious connection, characterized by patience, curiosity, and a deep respect for the body's adaptive wisdom. Full healing may not mean the complete absence of old patterns, but the presence of a compassionate awareness that knows how to navigate them when they arise.

The journey back to the body is not a gentle return to a safe harbor; it is the deliberate act of building a new harbor, plank by plank, in the midst of an ocean that is, and has always been, in motion. The ultimate challenge is not to finally understand your hunger, but to ask if you are willing to sit with the profound discomfort of not knowing, and to trust that a new kind of knowing can emerge from that very space.
